## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: Before using any chrome browser tools, you MUST first load them using ToolSearch.**

Chrome browser tools are MCP tools that require loading before use. Before calling any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tool:
1. Use ToolSearch with `select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__<tool_name>` to load the specific tool
2. Then call the tool

For example, to get tab context:
1. First: ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp"
2. Then: Call mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
CRITICAL: Before starting to work on a coding task, call the `initial_instructions` tool to read the 'Serena Instructions Manual'.

attachment.content:
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- controleur-fiscal: Inspecteur des finances publiques IA. Simule un contrôle fiscal DGFIP complet sur les comptes d'une
entreprise française (SASU, EURL, SAS, SARL). Analyse le FEC, la liasse fiscale, les charges déduites,
le compte courant d'associé, la TVA, l'IS selon 8 axes de vérification. Identifie les chefs de
redressement potentiels avec montants, base légale et niveaux de risque.

Triggers: contrôle fiscal, redressement, vérification comptabilité, DGFIP, FEC, déductibilité, audit fiscal, tax audit
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- fiscaliste: Fiscaliste IA pour la fiscalité personnelle des particuliers français : optimisation
et déclaration de l'impôt sur le revenu, IFI, revenus du capital, revenus fonciers,
equity salarial, crypto-actifs et PER.

Couvre le calcul de l'IR (barème, quotient familial, décote, PAS, CEHR, revenus
exceptionnels), la déclaration 2042 et annexes, les revenus du capital (PFU vs barème,
PEA, assurance-vie, dividendes, plus-values), les revenus fonciers (micro/réel,
déficit, LMNP, SCI IR), l'equity startup (RSU, BSPCE, stock-options, PEE/PERCO),
la fiscalité crypto (PAMC, 2086), l'IFI et les déductions (PER, pension alimentaire).

Triggers: impôt sur le revenu, IR, 2042, quotient familial, décote, PAS, PFU,
flat tax, PEA, assurance-vie, LMNP, revenus fonciers, déficit foncier, SCI IR, RSU,
BSPCE, stock-options, PEE/PERCO, crypto, 2086, IFI, PER, plafond PER, niche fiscale,
optimisation fiscale, simulation IR, TMI.

Hors scope : succession/donation (notaire), IS/SASU/arbitrage dividende-salaire/SCI IS
(comptable).
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- syndic: Gère un parc de copropriétés en France avec vue portfolio consolidée. Couvre administration,
comptabilité (décret 2005, plan comptable copro, 5 annexes), assemblées générales (convocation,
PV, notification), appels de fonds, travaux, fournisseurs, recouvrement d'impayés et transition
de syndic. Maîtrise les majorités (art. 24, 25, 25-1, 26), le fonds de travaux (art. 14-2),
le privilège immobilier (art. 19-2) et l'immatriculation RNC. Intégration Qonto pour le
rapprochement bancaire. Utilisé pour toute question liée à la copropriété, au syndic bénévole
ou coopératif, aux charges, tantièmes, AG, ou au droit de la copropriété (loi 1965, ALUR, ELAN).
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- commissaire-aux-comptes: Commissaire aux comptes IA pour l'audit des comptes annuels d'entreprises françaises. Applique la démarche
NEP en 7 phases : prise de connaissance, contrôle du FEC, vérification du bilan, du compte de résultat,
de la balance, de la liasse fiscale, et contrôles transversaux. Émet une opinion motivée sur la fiabilité
des comptes avec rapport structuré.

Triggers: audit, commissaire aux comptes, CAC, certification, comptes annuels, validation comptes, révision comptable, statutory audit
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- comptable: Comptabilité, fiscalité et facturation pour entreprises françaises. Gère écritures PCG, déclarations TVA, IS/IR, clôture annuelle, liasse fiscale (2033/2065), FEC, états financiers, et chaîne facturation (mentions obligatoires, numérotation, Factur-X/UBL/CII, plateformes agréées PDP/PA, e-reporting, réforme 2026, PEPPOL). Utiliser dès qu'une question porte sur comptabilité française, TVA, impôts, bilan, compte de résultat, amortissement, PCA, clôture, facture, avoir, devis, acompte, facturation électronique, ou e-invoicing.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- notaire: Notaire IA pour le droit immobilier, les successions, les donations, le droit de la famille
et le droit des sociétés en France. Copilote juridique pour la préparation d'actes, le conseil
patrimonial, les calculs de frais et la vérification de conformité.

Couvre le calcul des frais de notaire (DMTO, émoluments, débours, CSI), la plus-value
immobilière, les droits de succession et donation, le démembrement, les contrats de mariage,
les PACS, les SCI, et la rédaction de projets d'actes (compromis, statuts, testaments).

Triggers: notaire, frais de notaire, acte de vente, compromis, succession, donation, héritage, testament, PACS, contrat de mariage, SCI, plus-value immobilière, droits de mutation, DMTO, usufruit, nue-propriété, partage successoral, réserve héréditaire, viager, donation-partage, diagnostics immobilier, droit de préemption, acte notarié, droit immobilier
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- assistant: Routes to the correct sub-skill of the assistant family based on the issue's type:* and phase:* labels. Use when the user mentions a vendor onboarding or evolution and is unsure which skill to run.
- assistant-review: Review an open assistant PR against the dual-track checklist (dated documentation first, then code), with per-issue-type addenda for new vendors vs evolution. Use when the issue is at phase:review.
- assistant-propose: Open the right `type:new-assistant` / `type:vendor-evolution` GitHub issue from a free-form intent. Use when the user says they want to support / fix / evolve a vendor and has not yet filed an issue.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- assistant-implement: Scaffold (new-assistant) or refactor (vendor-evolution), document, test, and open a draft PR. Use when the issue is at phase:approved or phase:implementing and the contributor is starting or resuming work.
- assistant-merge: Verify gates one last time and squash-merge an assistant PR (new-assistant or vendor-evolution). Use when the issue is at phase:merge-ready.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- assistant-reverify: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, append a Change log entry, and either open a doc-only PR (no drift impact) or file a type:vendor-evolution issue (drift forces connector changes). Use when API drift is suspected.
- assistant-triage: Help the maintainer triage a phase:proposed assistant issue (new-assistant or vendor-evolution) — duplicate check, scope sanity, kind:* label application, decision draft. Use when reviewing an incoming proposal.
- assistant-tester-followup: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gem…) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

3	/// What `ClaudeCredentialLocator.locate()` produces — a Claude OAuth bearer
4	/// token resolved from the macOS Keychain entry written by the `claude` CLI.
5	public struct ClaudeCredentials: Sendable, Equatable {
6	    public let accessToken: String
7	
8	    public init(accessToken: String) {
9	        self.accessToken = accessToken
10	    }
11	}
12	
13	public enum ClaudeAuthError: Error, CustomStringConvertible {
14	    case keychainAccessDenied(serviceName: String, exitCode: Int32)
15	    case keychainEmpty(serviceName: String)
16	    case keychainTimeout(serviceName: String, timeoutSeconds: Int)
17	    case keychainParseFailed(serviceName: String, rawPreview: String)
18	
19	    public var description: String {
20	        switch self {
21	        case let .keychainAccessDenied(svc, code):
22	            "Keychain access denied for service '\(svc)' (exit \(code))"
23	        case let .keychainEmpty(svc):
24	            "Keychain item is empty for service '\(svc)'"
25	        case let .keychainTimeout(svc, secs):
26	            "Keychain access timed out after \(secs)s for service '\(svc)'"
27	        case let .keychainParseFailed(svc, preview):
28	            "Failed to parse keychain value for service '\(svc)' — preview: '\(preview.prefix(80))'"
29	        }
30	    }
31	}
32	
33	/// Reads the Claude OAuth access token from the macOS Keychain entry
34	/// written by the `claude` CLI. Read-only by contract — never calls
35	/// `SecItemAdd`, `SecItemUpdate`, `SecItemDelete`; never invokes
36	/// `claude auth login`; never writes any vendor file.
37	public actor ClaudeCredentialLocator: CredentialLocator {
38	    public typealias Credentials = ClaudeCredentials
39	
40	    public static let defaultKeychainService = "Claude Code-credentials"
41	    /// Long enough for a cached keychain lookup; short enough to avoid
42	    /// stalling the poller.
43	    public static let keychainTimeoutSeconds = 10
44	
45	    private let keychainServiceName: String
46	    private let processRunner: ProcessRunning
47	    private let logger: FileLogger
48	
49	    public init(
50	        keychainServiceName: String = ClaudeCredentialLocator.defaultKeychainService,
51	        processRunner: ProcessRunning = FoundationProcessRunner(),
52	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.claude
53	    ) {
54	        self.keychainServiceName = keychainServiceName
55	        self.processRunner = processRunner
56	        self.logger = logger
57	    }
58	
59	    public func locate() async throws -> ClaudeCredentials {
60	        let serviceName = keychainServiceName
61	        let timeoutSecs = Self.keychainTimeoutSeconds
62	
63	        let result = try await processRunner.run(
64	            executablePath: "/usr/bin/security",
65	            arguments: ["find-generic-password", "-s", serviceName, "-w"],
66	            timeoutSeconds: timeoutSecs
67	        )
68	        if result.timedOut {
69	            throw ClaudeAuthError.keychainTimeout(serviceName: serviceName, timeoutSeconds: timeoutSecs)
70	        }
71	        guard result.terminationStatus == 0 else {
72	            throw ClaudeAuthError.keychainAccessDenied(serviceName: serviceName, exitCode: result.terminationStatus)
73	        }
74	
75	        guard let raw = String(data: result.stdout, encoding: .utf8)?.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespacesAndNewlines),
76	              !raw.isEmpty else {
77	            throw ClaudeAuthError.keychainEmpty(serviceName: serviceName)
78	        }
79	
80	        guard let jsonData = raw.data(using: .utf8) else {
81	            throw ClaudeAuthError.keychainParseFailed(serviceName: serviceName, rawPreview: raw)
82	        }
83	
84	        let parsed: [String: Any]
85	        do {
86	            parsed = try JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: jsonData) as? [String: Any] ?? [:]
87	        } catch {
88	            throw ClaudeAuthError.keychainParseFailed(serviceName: serviceName, rawPreview: raw)
89	        }
90	
91	        guard let oauth = parsed["claudeAiOauth"] as? [String: Any],
92	              let token = oauth["accessToken"] as? String,
93	              !token.isEmpty else {
94	            throw ClaudeAuthError.keychainParseFailed(serviceName: serviceName, rawPreview: raw)
95	        }
96	
97	        return ClaudeCredentials(accessToken: token)
98	    }
99	}
100

content.thinking: I'm realizing the current approach only handles the Claude CLI keychain entry, but Claude Desktop stores credentials in a different location on macOS. I need to figure out where Desktop keeps its credentials and how to access them properly.

11	## Endpoints
12	
13	_verified: 2026-05-07_
14	
15	| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
16	|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
17	| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |
18	
19	The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
20	OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.
21	
22	## Credential sources
23	
24	_verified: 2026-05-07_
25	
26	Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):
27	
28	1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
29	   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
30	   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":...}}`.
31	
32	The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
33	`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
34	`~/.claude.json`.
35	
36	The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).
37	
38	## Sanitized fields
39	
40	_verified: 2026-05-07_
41	
42	Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.
43	
44	| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
45	|----------|-----------|-----------------|
46	| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
47	| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
48	| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
49	| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
50	| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
51	| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |
52	
53	The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
54	emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
55	schema changes.
56	
57	## Plan variants observed
58	
59	_verified: 2026-05-07_
60	
61	### Pro plan (verified by tester)
62	
63	```jsonc
64	<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
65	{
66	  "five_hour": {
67	    "utilization": 50.0,
68	    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
69	  },
70	  "seven_day": {
71	    "utilization": 13.0,
72	    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
73	  },

75	  "seven_day_opus": null,
76	  "seven_day_sonnet": {

78	    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
79	  },

81	  "seven_day_omelette": {
82	    "utilization": 0.0,
83	    "resets_at": null
84	  },

94	  }
95	}
96	```
97	
98	Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
99	`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
100	evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
101	windows it knows about.
102

104	
105	Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
106	instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.
107

109	
110	Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
111	and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.
112	
113	## Metric semantics
114	
115	_verified: 2026-05-07_
116	
117	| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
118	|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
119	| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
120	| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
121	| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
122	| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
123	
124	Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
125	Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
126	(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).
127	
128	## Time semantics
129	
130	_verified: 2026-05-07_
131	
132	- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
133	  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
134	- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
135	  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
136	  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.
137

139	
140	_verified: 2026-05-07_
141	
142	| Status | Connector response |
143	|--------|--------------------|
144	| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
145	| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
146	| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
147	| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |
148	
149	A `parse_error` is surfaced if the response cannot be decoded as JSON or
150	contains no known window block.

154	_verified: 2026-05-12_
155	
156	| URL | Format | Component filter |

160	`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
161	connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on

176	- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed

# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":...}}`.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if the response cannot be decoded as JSON or
contains no known window block.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.

| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if the response cannot be decoded as JSON or
contains no known window block.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.

# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":...}}`.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:

- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |

| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if the response cannot be decoded as JSON or
contains no known window block.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

content.thinking:
So the issue is clear now — the locator only reads the "Claude Code-credentials" keychain entry that the `claude` CLI writes, which means Claude Desktop users won't have this. I need to figure out what keychain service Claude Desktop actually uses for storing credentials, or if there's another way to access them locally.

I'm also realizing that account detection relies on `~/.claude.json`, which Claude Desktop users likely wouldn't have either. Let me trace through how the account detection actually works to understand what information is available to users in each scenario.

1	import Foundation
2	
3	public actor ClaudeCodeConnector: UsageConnector {
4	    nonisolated public let vendor: Vendor = .claude
5	
6	    private let claudeConfigPath: String
7	    private let logger: FileLogger
8	    private let session: URLSession
9	    private let credentialLocator: any CredentialLocator<ClaudeCredentials>
10	    private let tokenProvider: (@Sendable () async throws -> String)?
11	    private let accountProvider: (@Sendable () -> AccountEmail?)?
12	
13	    private var lastKnownMetrics: [UsageMetric] = []
14	
15	    // Claude Pro/Max rate-limit policy — durations are fixed by Anthropic and not returned by the API
16	    private static let sessionWindowMinutes = DurationMinutes(rawValue: 300)
17	    private static let weeklyWindowMinutes  = DurationMinutes(rawValue: 10080)
18	
19	    // Actor-isolated: safe without nonisolated(unsafe) since all access is on the actor
20	    private let isoFormatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
21	    private let isoFormatterFractional: ISO8601DateFormatter = {
22	        let f = ISO8601DateFormatter()
23	        f.formatOptions = [.withInternetDateTime, .withFractionalSeconds]
24	        return f
25	    }()
26	
27	
28	    public init(
29	        claudeConfigPath: String? = nil,
30	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.claude,
31	        session: URLSession = .shared,
32	        credentialLocator: any CredentialLocator<ClaudeCredentials> = ClaudeCredentialLocator(),
33	        tokenProvider: (@Sendable () async throws -> String)? = nil,
34	        accountProvider: (@Sendable () -> AccountEmail?)? = nil
35	    ) {
36	        let home = FileManager.default.homeDirectoryForCurrentUser.path
37	        self.claudeConfigPath = claudeConfigPath ?? "\(home)/.claude.json"
38	        self.logger = logger
39	        self.session = session
40	        self.credentialLocator = credentialLocator
41	        self.tokenProvider = tokenProvider
42	        self.accountProvider = accountProvider
43	    }
44	
45	    /// Legacy initializer preserved for tests that pre-date the credential
46	    /// locator extraction. Routes the `keychainServiceName` override into a
47	    /// freshly-built locator so the rest of the connector stays uniform.
48	    public init(
49	        claudeConfigPath: String? = nil,
50	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.claude,
51	        session: URLSession = .shared,
52	        keychainServiceName: String,
53	        tokenProvider: (@Sendable () async throws -> String)? = nil,
54	        accountProvider: (@Sendable () -> AccountEmail?)? = nil
55	    ) {
56	        self.init(
57	            claudeConfigPath: claudeConfigPath,
58	            logger: logger,
59	            session: session,
60	            credentialLocator: ClaudeCredentialLocator(
61	                keychainServiceName: keychainServiceName,
62	                logger: logger
63	            ),
64	            tokenProvider: tokenProvider,
65	            accountProvider: accountProvider
66	        )
67	    }
68	
69	    // MARK: - UsageConnector
70	
71	    nonisolated public func resolveActiveAccount() -> AccountEmail? {
72	        if let provider = accountProvider {
73	            return provider()
74	        }
75	        guard let data = FileManager.default.contents(atPath: claudeConfigPath) else {
76	            logger.log(.warning, "Cannot read \(claudeConfigPath)")
77	            return nil
78	        }
79	        do {
80	            let json = try JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: data) as? [String: Any]
81	            let oauth = json?["oauthAccount"] as? [String: Any]
82	            return (oauth?["emailAddress"] as? String).map(AccountEmail.init(rawValue:))
83	        } catch {
84	            logger.log(.error, "Failed to parse \(claudeConfigPath): \(error)")
85	            return nil
86	        }
87	    }
88	
89	    public func fetchUsages() async throws -> [VendorUsageEntry] {
90	        guard let earlyAccount = resolveActiveAccount() else {
91	            logger.log(.warning, "Cannot resolve active account — skipping fetch")
92	            return [errorEntry(account: "unknown", type: "account_unknown")]
93	        }
94	        logger.log(.info, "Fetching usages for account=\(earlyAccount)")
95	
96	        let token: String
97	        do {
98	            if let provider = tokenProvider {
99	                token = try await provider()
100	            } else {
101	                token = try await credentialLocator.locate().accessToken
102	            }
103	        } catch {
104	            logger.log(.error, "Token retrieval failed: \(error)")
105	            return [errorEntry(account: earlyAccount, type: "token_error")]
106	        }
107	
108	        // Re-read at HTTP dispatch time: if the user switched accounts while the token was being
109	        // fetched, the response must be attributed to whoever was active when the request went out.
110	        let account = resolveActiveAccount() ?? earlyAccount
111	
112	        guard let url = URL(string: "https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage") else {
113	            return [errorEntry(account: account, type: "api_error")]
114	        }
115	        var request = URLRequest(url: url)
116	        request.setValue("Bearer \(token)", forHTTPHeaderField: "Authorization")
117	        // Required by the OAuth usage endpoint — beta flag must match the rollout date
118	        request.setValue("oauth-2025-04-20", forHTTPHeaderField: "anthropic-beta")
119	        request.timeoutInterval = 5
120	
121	        let data: Data
122	        let response: URLResponse
123	        do {
124	            (data, response) = try await session.data(for: request)
125	        } catch {
126	            logger.log(.error, "API request failed: \(error)")
127	            return [errorEntry(account: account, type: "api_error")]
128	        }
129	
130	        let httpCode = (response as? HTTPURLResponse)?.statusCode ?? -1
131	        logger.log(.info, "API response: HTTP \(httpCode)")
132	
133	        if httpCode == 429 {
134	            let body = String(data: data, encoding: .utf8) ?? "<non-UTF8 body, \(data.count) bytes>"
135	            logger.log(.warning, "API rate-limited (HTTP 429): \(body)")
136	            return [errorEntry(account: account, type: "http_429", isActive: true, preservedMetrics: lastKnownMetrics)]
137	        }
138	
139	        guard let http = response as? HTTPURLResponse, http.statusCode == 200 else {
140	            logger.log(.error, "API returned HTTP \(httpCode)")
141	            return [errorEntry(account: account, type: "http_\(httpCode)")]
142	        }
143	
144	        logger.log(.debug, "API payload: \(Self.maskedPayload(data))")
145	
146	        do {
147	            let usage = try parseAPIResponse(data)
148	            logger.log(.info, "Fetched successfully: session=\(usage.sessionPercent.map { "\($0)%" } ?? "nil") weekly=\(usage.weeklyPercent.map { "\($0)%" } ?? "nil") sonnet=\(usage.sonnetWeekly.map { "\($0.percent)%" } ?? "nil") opus=\(usage.opusWeekly.map { "\($0.percent)%" } ?? "nil")")
149	            // A window is emitted whenever utilization is known, even if resets_at is null
150	            // (fresh account, between cycles). A null reset date surfaces in the UI as "???"
151	            // rather than silently omitting the metric.
152	            var metrics: [UsageMetric] = []
153	            if let percent = usage.sessionPercent {
154	                metrics.append(.timeWindow(
155	                    name: "5h sessions (all models)",
156	                    resetAt: usage.sessionResetAt,
157	                    windowDuration: Self.sessionWindowMinutes,
158	                    usagePercent: UsagePercent(rawValue: percent)
159	                ))
160	            }
161	            if let percent = usage.weeklyPercent {
162	                metrics.append(.timeWindow(
163	                    name: "Weekly (all models)",
164	                    resetAt: usage.weeklyResetAt,
165	                    windowDuration: Self.weeklyWindowMinutes,
166	                    usagePercent: UsagePercent(rawValue: percent)
167	                ))
168	            }
169	            // Per-model weekly metrics are purely additive — absent keys are silently skipped
170	            let perModelDefs: [(metric: ModelWeeklyMetric?, name: String)] = [
171	                (usage.sonnetWeekly, "Weekly Sonnet"),
172	                (usage.opusWeekly, "Weekly Opus"),
173	            ]
174	            for def in perModelDefs {
175	                if let m = def.metric {
176	                    metrics.append(.timeWindow(
177	                        name: def.name,
178	                        resetAt: m.resetAt,
179	                        windowDuration: Self.weeklyWindowMinutes,
180	                        usagePercent: UsagePercent(rawValue: m.percent)
181	                    ))
182	                }
183	            }
184	            lastKnownMetrics = metrics
185	            return [VendorUsageEntry(
186	                vendor: vendor,
187	                account: account,
188	                isActive: true,
189	                lastAcquiredOn: ISODate(date: Date()),
190	                lastError: nil,
191	                metrics: metrics
192	            )]
193	        } catch {
194	            logger.log(.error, "Response parse failed: \(error)")
195	            logger.log(.warning, "Failed payload dump: \(Self.maskedPayload(data))")
196	            return [errorEntry(account: account, type: "parse_error")]
197	        }
198	    }
199	
200	    // MARK: - Response parsing
201	
202	    private static let sensitiveKeyPatterns = ["token", "key", "secret", "password", "email", "credential"]
203	
204	    private static func maskedPayload(_ data: Data) -> String {
205	        guard let json = try? JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: data) as? [String: Any] else {
206	            return "<non-JSON, \(data.count) bytes>"
207	        }
208	        let masked = maskSensitiveFields(json)
209	        guard let out = try? JSONSerialization.data(withJSONObject: masked, options: [.sortedKeys]),
210	              let str = String(data: out, encoding: .utf8) else {
211	            return "<serialization failed>"
212	        }
213	        return str
214	    }
215	
216	    private static func maskSensitiveFields(_ dict: [String: Any]) -> [String: Any] {
217	        var result: [String: Any] = [:]
218	        for (key, value) in dict {
219	            let lower = key.lowercased()
220	            if sensitiveKeyPatterns.contains(where: { lower.contains($0) }) {
221	                result[key] = "***"
222	            } else if let nested = value as? [String: Any] {
223	                result[key] = maskSensitiveFields(nested)
224	            } else if let array = value as? [[String: Any]] {
225	                result[key] = array.map { maskSensitiveFields($0) }
226	            } else {
227	                result[key] = value
228	            }
229	        }
230	        return result
231	    }
232	
233	    private struct ModelWeeklyMetric {
234	        let percent: Int
235	        let resetAt: ISODate?
236	    }
237	
238	    private struct ParsedUsage {
239	        // Every window is optional: a freshly provisioned account (or an account
240	        // between reset cycles) returns `utilization: 0, resets_at: null` and must
241	        // not be treated as a parse failure.
242	        let sessionPercent: Int?
243	        let sessionResetAt: ISODate?
244	        let weeklyPercent: Int?
245	        let weeklyResetAt: ISODate?
246	        let sonnetWeekly: ModelWeeklyMetric?
247	        let opusWeekly: ModelWeeklyMetric?
248	    }
249	
250	    private func parseAPIResponse(_ data: Data) throws -> ParsedUsage {
251	        let jsonObject = try JSONSerialization.jsonObject(with: data)
252	        guard let json = jsonObject as? [String: Any] else {
253	            throw ConnectorError.unexpectedAPIFormat(receivedKeys: [])
254	        }
255	        // Unknown top-level keys are ignored by design — the API evolves with experimental
256	        // fields (e.g. `omelette_promotional`, `iguana_necktie`) and we only consume the
257	        // windows we know about. Only a truly malformed payload (no known window at all)
258	        // escalates to an error.
259	        let session = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "five_hour")
260	        let weekly = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "seven_day")
261	        let sonnet = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "seven_day_sonnet")
262	        let opus = extractOptionalModelMetric(from: json, key: "seven_day_opus")
263	
264	        let hasFiveHourBlock = json["five_hour"] is [String: Any]
265	        let hasSevenDayBlock = json["seven_day"] is [String: Any]
266	        if !hasFiveHourBlock, !hasSevenDayBlock {
267	            logger.log(.warning, "No known usage window in payload — top-level keys: \(Array(json.keys))")
268	            throw ConnectorError.unexpectedAPIFormat(receivedKeys: Array(json.keys))
269	        }
270	
271	        return ParsedUsage(
272	            sessionPercent: session.map { Int($0.percent) },
273	            sessionResetAt: session.flatMap { $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) } },
274	            weeklyPercent: weekly.map { Int($0.percent) },
275	            weeklyResetAt: weekly.flatMap { $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) } },
276	            sonnetWeekly: sonnet.map { ModelWeeklyMetric(percent: $0.percent, resetAt: $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) }) },
277	            opusWeekly: opus.map { ModelWeeklyMetric(percent: $0.percent, resetAt: $0.resetAt.map { normalizeISO8601($0) }) }
278	        )
279	    }
280	
281	    /// Every usage window block is optional — a missing key or an unexpected shape is silently
282	    /// skipped. A null `resets_at` (fresh account, between cycles) is kept with `resetAt: nil`
283	    /// so the metric still surfaces in the UI as "???".
284	    private func extractOptionalModelMetric(from json: [String: Any], key: String) -> (percent: Int, resetAt: String?)? {
285	        guard let block = json[key] as? [String: Any] else {
286	            return nil
287	        }
288	        guard let util = block["utilization"] as? Double else {
289	            logger.log(.debug, "Block '\(key)' present but utilization missing — skipped")
290	            return nil
291	        }
292	        let resetAt = block["resets_at"] as? String
293	        if resetAt == nil {
294	            logger.log(.debug, "Block '\(key)' has null resets_at — metric emitted without reset date")
295	        }
296	        return (percent: Int(util.rounded()), resetAt: resetAt)
297	    }
298	
299	    /// Strips sub-second precision from API-provided ISO8601 strings so downstream
300	    /// consumers can rely on the standard formatter (no fractional-seconds option needed).
301	    /// Returns the original string unchanged if it cannot be parsed.
302	    private func normalizeISO8601(_ raw: String) -> ISODate {
303	        if let date = isoFormatterFractional.date(from: raw) ?? isoFormatter.date(from: raw) {
304	            return ISODate(rawValue: isoFormatter.string(from: date))
305	        }
306	        return ISODate(rawValue: raw)
307	    }
308	
309	    // MARK: - Helpers
310	
311	    private func errorEntry(account: AccountEmail, type: String, isActive: Bool = false, preservedMetrics: [UsageMetric] = []) -> VendorUsageEntry {
312	        return VendorUsageEntry(
313	            vendor: vendor,
314	            account: account,
315	            isActive: isActive,
316	            lastAcquiredOn: nil,
317	            lastError: UsageError(timestamp: ISODate(date: Date()), type: type),
318	            metrics: preservedMetrics
319	        )
320	    }
321	}
322	
323	public enum ConnectorError: Error, CustomStringConvertible {
324	    case keychainAccessDenied(serviceName: String, exitCode: Int32)
325	    case keychainEmpty(serviceName: String)
326	    case keychainTimeout(serviceName: String, timeoutSeconds: Int)
327	    case tokenParseError(rawValue: String)
328	    case unexpectedAPIFormat(receivedKeys: [String])
329	
330	    public var description: String {
331	        switch self {
332	        case let .keychainAccessDenied(svc, code):
333	            "Keychain access denied for service '\(svc)' (exit \(code))"
334	        case let .keychainEmpty(svc):
335	            "Keychain item is empty for service '\(svc)'"
336	        case let .keychainTimeout(svc, secs):
337	            "Keychain access timed out after \(secs)s for service '\(svc)'"
338	        case let .tokenParseError(raw):
339	            "Failed to parse OAuth token — raw value preview: '\(raw.prefix(80))'"
340	        case let .unexpectedAPIFormat(keys):
341	            "API response does not match expected format — top-level keys: \(keys)"
342	        }
343	    }
344	}

content.thinking:
Now I'm seeing the full picture — the user's hypothesis checks out. Their usages.json shows they're actively working with 84% utilization on 5-hour sessions, but they're saying other users have nothing in their files. Looking back at their message more carefully, they shared one user's usages.json as a reference point, though I'm not entirely sure if that's meant to show a working example or demonstrate what happens when something breaks.

Re-reading their complaint: users are reporting that when they only use Claude Desktop (not the CLI), their time window never starts in the app, even though they see consumption in Desktop itself. That's odd — why would they show me a working file then? Unless this file actually belongs to one of those affected users, where the 84% metric looks current but never updates to reflect Desktop usage.

But here's what's really telling: the lastError shows `http_429` from today (May 19, 2026), while lastAcquiredOn is from April 27 — almost a month ago. The token worked once back then when they used the CLI, but since then there's been no successful refresh. A 429 error means rate-limited, not a token problem, so the token itself is being read. The metrics at 84% are stale from that April date, and the resetAt timestamp is also from April — way past. So the pattern is clear: they logged in successfully with the CLI back in April, got a valid token, but now the API keeps returning rate-limit errors.

The real question is why 429 specifically. I'm wondering if the API returns that error when someone's using a Claude Code OAuth token to query usage but only consuming through Claude Desktop, since those are different OAuth apps. Or maybe the token is scoped only to Claude Code. The user's complaint about the time window never starting makes sense now — the resetAt date never gets updated to a future value because the API call keeps failing with 429.

Looking at the timestamps, the last successful token acquisition was April 27, but the last error is from today (May 19), so this has been broken for about three weeks. My best guess: the OAuth refresh token might need an active re-login through the CLI to refresh, and since the user only uses Claude Desktop, that token never gets refreshed and eventually expires, causing the 429 responses.

The practical next steps are to check what keychain entry Claude Desktop actually uses, then modify the credential locator to cascade through both the CLI and Desktop entries. I should also ask the user to dump their keychain entries and check the app logs to see if token retrieval is actually failing.

Je vois que le fichier usages.json présenté ne correspond pas exactement à ce que l'utilisateur décrivait — c'est un cas où le token est valide mais l'API retourne quand même une erreur 429. Je vais consolider ma réponse plutôt que de continuer à analyser en profondeur, même si l'utilisateur a peut-être fait une confusion ou voulait simplement me montrer un exemple.

En creusant plus, je réalise que si Claude Desktop était utilisé sans CLI installé, on verrait une erreur `token_error` au lieu de 429. Et si le CLI avait été installé puis abandonné, on attendrait un `token_expired` (401), pas 429. L'exemple fourni ne valide donc pas vraiment l'hypothèse de l'utilisateur — il faut clarifier ce qui se passe réellement.

Ce qui me frappe surtout, c'est que la personne dans cet exemple a clairement utilisé le CLI au moins une fois puisqu'elle a une entrée avec un email enregistré et une clé keychain. Ce qui s'est probablement passé, c'est qu'elle a arrêté d'utiliser le CLI mais continue avec Claude Desktop, et le token OAuth du CLI ne reflète que l'usage de Claude Code lui-même — l'endpoint d'usage est limité au client OAuth qui a généré le token.

Le vrai problème semble être que depuis 3 semaines, l'API répond 429, ce qui empêche la fenêtre de temps de se mettre à jour. Le `resetAt` du 27 avril reste figé dans le passé parce qu'on ne reçoit jamais une réponse 200 avec un nouveau timestamp. Je dois investiguer pourquoi cette 429 persiste — ça pourrait être un ban IP, un problème d'usage global Anthropic, ou autre chose.

content.text:
J'ai investigué le flux. Voici ce que je vois et pourquoi ton hypothèse mérite d'être nuancée avant de coder quoi que ce soit.

## Ce que fait l'app aujourd'hui

`ClaudeCredentialLocator.locate()` lit **uniquement** l'entrée keychain `Claude Code-credentials` (écrite par le CLI `claude`). Et `resolveActiveAccount()` lit l'email depuis `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`). → Donc oui, ces deux sources sont 100 % des artefacts du CLI Claude Code, jamais de Claude Desktop.

## Ce que dit le fichier `usages.json` que tu m'as donné

Il ne valide PAS l'hypothèse "token introuvable", parce que :

1. **`account: "robin.lopez@4sh.fr"`** → `~/.claude.json` existe et est parsé. Sinon on aurait `account: "unknown"` + `lastError.type: "account_unknown"` (cf. `ClaudeCodeConnector.swift:92`).
2. **`lastError.type: "http_429"`** → la requête HTTP est partie avec un Bearer token. Si le keychain était vide/inaccessible, on verrait `token_error` (`ClaudeCodeConnector.swift:105`), pas `http_429`.
3. **`lastAcquiredOn: 2026-04-27` + `resets_at: 2026-04-27T13:39Z`** → la dernière réponse 200 date du 27 avril. Depuis ~3 semaines on est en 429 permanent (le `lastKnownMetrics` est préservé exprès en 429, cf. ligne 136).

Donc pour cet utilisateur Robin : **le token est trouvé, l'API est appelée, mais Anthropic renvoie 429 systématiquement.** La fenêtre "ne démarre jamais" parce qu'on conserve les métriques staled sans pouvoir les rafraîchir.

## Hypothèses plus probables que le token introuvable

- **A — Token Claude Code stale** : Robin a utilisé le CLI une fois (avril), n'y est plus retourné. Le CLI ne tourne plus → pas de refresh token. L'access token est expiré mais Anthropic renvoie bizarrement 429 au lieu de 401. Possible mais étrange.
- **B — Endpoint OAuth scopé au client** : `api/oauth/usage` retourne probablement l'usage du **client OAuth qui détient le token**, pas l'usage global du compte. Un token "Claude Code" ne verrait jamais l'usage "Claude Desktop". Mais ça donnerait des 200 avec utilisation à 0, pas du 429.
- **C — Rate-limit côté Anthropic** sur le token CLI inactif (suspicious activity / fraud detection) → 429 jusqu'à re-login CLI.
- **D — Ton hypothèse** : pour les utilisateurs qui n'ont **jamais** utilisé le CLI, là on aurait `account_unknown` ou `token_error`, pas du 429. Il y a peut-être deux populations distinctes : (a) ceux qui n'ont jamais CLI → erreurs duremes (b) ceux comme Robin → 429.

## Ce dont j'ai besoin pour trancher

Peux-tu me donner, sur les utilisateurs concernés :

1. Les **logs `claude.log`** (`~/Library/Logs/AIUsagesTrackers/` ou équivalent dans ton app) pour voir le détail HTTP + body du 429 et confirmer la cascade `Fetching usages → Token retrieval → API response`.
2. Sur la machine d'un user affecté, le résultat de :
   ```sh
   security find-generic-password -s "Claude Code-credentials" -w | head -c 30
   ls -la ~/.claude.json
   security dump-keychain -d login.keychain 2>/dev/null | grep -i -E "claude|anthropic" | grep svce
   ```
   Le dernier point identifierait le **nom du service keychain utilisé par Claude Desktop** — c'est précisément ce qu'il faut savoir pour étendre `ClaudeCredentialLocator` en cascade (CLI → Desktop).
3. Le `usages.json` d'un user qui a **vraiment** jamais touché le CLI (devrait montrer `account_unknown` ou `token_error`, pas 429).

Dis-moi ce que tu trouves et on décide de la suite — soit étendre le locator pour lire l'entrée keychain Claude Desktop (si elle existe et expose un OAuth utilisable), soit traiter spécifiquement les 429 staled (forcer une réauth après N jours).

content.thinking: The user is showing me a screenshot where the app is displaying "???" instead of the expected content, so I need to examine the image to understand what's going wrong.

Je remarque une incohérence : la capture d'écran affiche des `???` pour `usagePercent` et `resetAt`, alors que le fichier `usages.json` que tu m'as fourni contient clairement `usagePercent: 84` et `resetAt: 2026-04-27T13:39:59Z`. Il y a une discordance entre ces deux sources de données.

Plusieurs hypothèses pourraient expliquer cela : soit c'est un utilisateur différent malgré le même email, soit l'interface affiche `???` quand `resetAt` est dépassé, ou peut-être quand un `lastError` est présent pour éviter d'afficher des données obsolètes. Je vais examiner le code de rendu de l'UI pour comprendre ce comportement.

content.text: Les `???` viennent du rendu UI, pas des données. Le `usages.json` que tu m'as donné a pourtant `usagePercent: 84` et un `resetAt`. Donc soit l'UI masque les valeurs quand `resetAt` est passé (or il l'est, 27 avril), soit quand `lastError` est présent. Laisse-moi vérifier.

content.thinking:
Found it — the rendering checks `state.isUnknown`, which comes from `UsageComputations` and returns true when `resetAt` is either nil or in the past. There's even a test case confirming this behavior: when `resetAt` is in the past, both the percentage and remaining values display as `???`.

Looking at the actual data, `resetAt` is set to April 27, 2026, but we're now in May 19 — so it's definitely in the past, which explains why everything shows as `???`. This isn't actually a token problem; the token and API worked fine on April 27. Since then, repeated 429 errors have prevented `resetAt` from refreshing, causing it to expire and triggering the `???` display.

The real issue is figuring out why the 429 errors keep happening. The user's suggestion about needing to fetch the token from Claude Desktop is still just a hypothesis. But rethinking what they said — they mentioned that when users only use Claude Desktop and never touch the CLI, their usage window doesn't start in the app even though they see consumption in Desktop itself. That 429 pattern wouldn't occur for someone who's never used the CLI at all; they'd get a `token_error` instead since there's no keychain entry.

So maybe Robin does have a Claude Code keychain entry, but it's returning an expired token, which would explain the 429 — though that seems odd since an expired token should return 401 instead. Or possibly `claude.ai/code` uses the same `oauth/usage` endpoint but with a different token altogether.

The key insight: if Claude Desktop refreshes its own token while the Claude Code CLI token in the keychain never gets refreshed, those credentials just expire over time. The 429 response might be Anthropic's way of discouraging polling when no Claude Code usage has been recorded in a while, rather than a true auth failure.

So the real takeaway is that the `???` display isn't from a missing token — the data exists but `resetAt` is stale, which is why the UI shows unknown. The root cause of the 429 errors over the past three weeks still needs investigation through HTTP logs.

content.text:
Confirmé : ce que tu vois sur la capture **n'est pas dû au token**. C'est le rendu UI qui dégrade en `???` quand `resetAt` est dans le passé.

## Mécanique exacte

`TimeWindowMetricRow.swift:37,48,52` :
```swift
Text(state.isUnknown ? "???" : "\(usagePercent.rawValue)%")
Text(state.isUnknown ? "???" : formatRemainingTime(...))
Text(state.isUnknown ? "resets ???" : "resets \(formatResetDate(...))")
```

`state.isUnknown` passe à `true` notamment quand `resetAt` est dans le passé — cf. test `UsageStoreTests.swift:236` "resetAt in the past: both percent and remaining render as ???".

## Ce qui se passe pour Robin

Dans son `usages.json` :
- `resetAt: 2026-04-27T13:39:59Z` → on est le 19 mai, donc largement dépassé
- `lastError.type: http_429` depuis le 19 mai → `lastKnownMetrics` est conservé tel quel (cf. `ClaudeCodeConnector.swift:136`), mais avec un `resetAt` mort

Résultat : l'UI a bien les 84 % en mémoire, mais comme la date de reset est dépassée et qu'on ne peut plus la rafraîchir (429 systématique), elle bascule en `???`. Le badge `active` vient probablement du fait que `isActive: true` est forcé en 429.

## Donc la racine du problème n'est PAS le token

C'est le **HTTP 429 persistant pendant 3 semaines**. Tant qu'on ne sait pas pourquoi Anthropic renvoie 429 sur le token CLI de Robin, l'idée "lire le token Claude Desktop" est prématurée — et ne réglerait peut-être rien si l'endpoint `api/oauth/usage` ne reconnaît pas un token Desktop (il est scopé "Claude Code").

## Prochaines actions concrètes

Sur la machine de Robin, j'aurais besoin de :

1. **Le `claude.log`** pour voir le body du 429 :
   ```sh
   tail -200 ~/Library/Logs/AIUsagesTrackers/claude.log
   ```
   (ou le chemin que tu utilises — dis-moi si différent)

2. **Tester manuellement le token** :
   ```sh
   TOKEN=$(security find-generic-password -s "Claude Code-credentials" -w | jq -r .claudeAiOauth.accessToken)
   curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
        -H "anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20" \
        https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage
   ```
   Si ça répond 200 → bug dans l'app. Si ça répond 429/401 → problème côté Anthropic / token stale.

3. **Lui faire lancer `claude` une fois dans le CLI** (juste se logger, pas besoin d'en faire plus) pour forcer un refresh, puis retester. Si après ça l'app remonte les valeurs, on a la confirmation que c'est une stagnation du token CLI faute de refresh.

Veux-tu qu'on parte là-dessus ou tu préfères qu'on ajoute aussi un comportement défensif côté app (par exemple : surfacer un état "stale" différencié quand `resetAt` est dépassé ET qu'on a une erreur depuis > N jours, pour éviter ce mode "tout `???` silencieux") ?
