file.content:
# Build it yourself

This guide walks a non-developer reader through cloning a pull request
and producing the same `.app` the maintainer attaches to an onboarding
issue. Use it when you want to validate a tester DMG before installing
it, or when you would rather not trust an ad-hoc-signed prebuilt.

The whole path takes ~5 minutes plus the time the build itself takes
(~2-5 minutes on a recent Mac).

## 1. Prerequisites

- **Xcode** — the version pinned by the package manifest. Install from
  the Mac App Store if you do not already have it. Open Xcode once after
  installing so it agrees to its license; on first build it may ask to
  download additional components.
- **macOS 15 or later**.
- **Git**. macOS ships a recent enough version; running `xcode-select
  --install` installs the developer command-line tools if needed.
- (Optional) **GitHub CLI** (`gh`) — convenient for checking out a PR by
  number. Install via `brew install gh` or skip and use the ZIP path
  below.

## 2. Get the source

### With `gh` (recommended)

```sh
gh repo clone fcamblor/ai-usages-tracker
cd ai-usages-tracker
gh pr checkout <pr-number>
```

`<pr-number>` is the number shown in the PR URL on GitHub.

### Without `gh`

On the PR's GitHub page, click **Code → Download ZIP** of the source
branch (the dropdown next to the green "Code" button on the PR's branch).
Unzip it and `cd` into the resulting directory.

## 3. Build

From the repository root:

```sh
./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
```

The script compiles a release build, assembles `dist/AI Usages
Tracker.app`, and ad-hoc-signs it. Output lines starting with `→` mark
each step.

To reproduce the **tester-debug** mode locally (the one CI produces for
the build attached to a tester issue), set the same three environment
variables CI sets:

```sh
VENDOR_DEBUG=<vendor-slug> \
BUILD_COMMIT=$(git rev-parse HEAD) \
ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL=https://github.com/fcamblor/ai-usages-tracker/issues/<n> \
  ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
```

`<vendor-slug>` is the lowercase identifier (e.g. `claude`, `codex`,
`copilot`) for the vendor under test. The three keys land in the produced
bundle's Info.plist; without them, the in-app tester feedback banner is
invisible by construction.

## 4. Run

```sh
open "dist/AI Usages Tracker.app"
```

If macOS refuses to launch the app because it is not from an identified
developer, **right-click** the `.app` in Finder and choose **Open**, then
confirm the dialog. This is the standard Gatekeeper bypass for ad-hoc
signed binaries — you only need to do it once.

## 5. (Optional) Compare against the maintainer's DMG

If you want to confirm the maintainer's prebuilt DMG matches what you
just built locally, compute its SHA-256:

```sh
shasum -a 256 ~/Downloads/AI-Usages-Tracker-<vendor>-<sha8>.dmg
```

The same value is published in the sticky build comment on the PR and on
the linked issue.

Note: Swift release builds are not strictly bit-for-bit reproducible
across machines and toolchain versions, so a SHA mismatch does not by
itself indicate tampering. The trust path here is "you read the source
and built it yourself"; SHA matching is a bonus when it works.

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

content.subject: Add expiresAt check in ClaudeCredentialLocator
content.description: Parse expiresAt from the keychain JSON; if expired (with small skew margin) throw a dedicated tokenExpired error before any HTTP call.

content.subject: Handle token_expired explicitly in ClaudeCodeConnector
content.description: Map ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired and HTTP 401 to lastError.type "token_expired", clear lastKnownMetrics, do not preserve stale metrics.

content.subject: Update tests for new token expiry paths
content.description: Add tests covering expired keychain JSON → tokenExpired error, HTTP 401 → token_expired entry with cleared metrics.

content.subject: Update docs/vendors/claude.md
content.description: Document expiresAt field in keychain JSON, local pre-check semantics, and 401 → token_expired behavior with cleared metrics. Bump verified dates and add changelog entry.

content.subject: Preserve metrics on token_expired, persist auth signal across 429s
content.description: Revert lastKnownMetrics wipe on 401 and tokenExpired. Add actor-private lastAuthFailureSeen flag set on 401/tokenExpired, cleared on 200. While set, treat HTTP 429 as token_expired so the auth signal isn't masked by the 429 cascade.
content.activeForm: Preserving metrics and persisting auth signal

content.subject: Update tests and docs for preserved metrics behavior
content.description: Adjust the new tests so they expect preserved metrics on 401/tokenExpired. Add a test for the 401 → 429 cascade keeping token_expired. Update docs/vendors/claude.md.

content.subject: Add guard tests against accidental window deletion on token expiry
content.description: Tests that lock down per-metric value preservation (name, percent, resetAt, duration) across token_expired and 401→429 cascades, with resetAt explicitly in the far future so any regression that drops or alters non-expired windows fails loudly.

file.content:
name: Assistant change nightly build

# Builds an ad-hoc-signed DMG for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant`
# or `type:vendor-evolution` issue, with tester-debug mode active for the
# vendor under test. Posts a sticky comment on the linked issue (the
# canonical tester gathering point). See docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md §6.

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, reopened, synchronize, ready_for_review, closed]
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      pr_number:
        description: PR number to build
        required: true

concurrency:
  group: assistant-build-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.event.inputs.pr_number }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write
  issues: write

# The build job overrides `contents` to `write` because it publishes a
# prerelease with the DMG attached. We keep the top-level permission at
# `read` so resolve-issue runs with least privilege.

jobs:
  resolve-issue:
    if: github.event.action != 'closed' && github.event.pull_request.draft != true
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      in_scope: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.in_scope }}
      issue_number: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.issue_number }}
      issue_url: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.issue_url }}
      vendor_slug: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.vendor_slug }}
      pr_number: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.pr_number }}
      head_sha: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.head_sha }}
      head_sha_short: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.head_sha_short }}
      head_ref: ${{ steps.detect.outputs.head_ref }}
    steps:
      - name: Detect linked issue and vendor slug
        id: detect
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          REPO: ${{ github.repository }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.event.inputs.pr_number }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          pr_json=$(gh api "repos/${REPO}/pulls/${PR_NUMBER}")
          body=$(printf '%s' "$pr_json" | jq -r '.body // ""')
          head_sha=$(printf '%s' "$pr_json" | jq -r '.head.sha')
          head_ref=$(printf '%s' "$pr_json" | jq -r '.head.ref')
          short=${head_sha:0:8}

          # Match `Closes #<n>` (case-insensitive, allow Fixes/Resolves too).
          issue_number=$(printf '%s' "$body" \
            | grep -Eio '(closes|fixes|resolves)[[:space:]]+#[0-9]+' \

content.thinking:
Je vois que le workflow `assistant-build.yml` génère automatiquement un DMG signé pour les PRs avec les labels `type:new-assistant` ou `type:vendor-evolution`, et notre changement sur le connecteur Claude pour gérer une nouvelle erreur correspond effectivement à ce dernier scope — donc on devrait être couvert par ce workflow.

Si on crée une issue avec ce label et qu'on lie la PR, le workflow construit et publie automatiquement un DMG en attachement. Sinon, localement, je peux utiliser `./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` pour générer un `.app` dans `dist/`, que je peux zipper et partager. Je vais aussi vérifier le workflow de release pour comprendre le processus public.

There's no dedicated DMG build script locally — the DMG only gets generated through CI in the release workflow. For sharing locally, I have two options: either use the automatic CI path by creating an issue with the `type:vendor-evolution` label and opening a PR with `Closes #<n>`, which triggers the `assistant-build.yml` workflow to produce and post the DMG in a comment, or handle it manually some other way.

Actually, looking at the next part — there's a local script I can use to build the app bundle, zip it up, and send it over, though that requires the recipient to manually bypass Gatekeeper. I'll present both approaches to Robin and let him choose which works better for him.

content.text:
Deux voies, selon que tu veux passer par la chaîne CI officielle ou bricoler localement.

## Voie 1 — CI : DMG signé ad-hoc, publié sur l'issue (recommandée)

Le repo a un workflow `assistant-build.yml` qui produit automatiquement un DMG pour toute PR liée à une issue labellisée `type:vendor-evolution`, avec le mode tester-debug activé. C'est la voie prévue pour ce genre de fix.

Process :

1. Ouvre une issue `type:vendor-evolution` pour ce fix (slug vendeur `claude`). La skill `assistant-propose` fait ça si tu veux passer par elle.
2. Pousse la branche, ouvre la PR avec `Closes #<n>` dans la description.
3. Le workflow se déclenche et poste un commentaire collant avec un lien vers un DMG `AI-Usages-Tracker-claude-<sha8>.dmg` (un prerelease GitHub).
4. Tu envoies ce lien à Robin. Il télécharge, double-clic, drag vers `/Applications`. Au premier lancement il fait **clic droit → Ouvrir** pour passer Gatekeeper (signature ad-hoc).

Avantage : Robin obtient un binaire reproductible, attaché à l'issue, avec le banner tester-debug actif qui lui permet de cliquer "remonter un retour" et d'attacher ses logs sanitizés directement.

## Voie 2 — Local : .app zippé envoyé directement

Plus rapide si Robin est de confiance et tu veux juste un test ponctuel.

```sh
./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
# produit "dist/AI Usages Tracker.app"
ditto -c -k --keepParent "dist/AI Usages Tracker.app" /tmp/AIUsagesTracker.zip
```

Tu lui envoies le `.zip` (Slack, Drive…). Il décompresse, déplace dans `/Applications`, et au premier lancement **clic droit → Ouvrir** (Gatekeeper).

Si tu veux activer le banner tester-debug pour qu'il puisse remonter ses logs proprement :

```sh
VENDOR_DEBUG=claude \
BUILD_COMMIT=$(git rev-parse HEAD) \
ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL=https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<n> \
  ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
```

## Recommandation

Vu qu'on veut surtout vérifier que le fix marche chez Robin (et collecter des logs si ça remarche pas), **voie 1**. L'issue+PR donnent un cadre pour son retour, et la skill `assistant-tester-followup` côté toi structurera son sign-off.

Côté pratique pour Robin, dis-lui aussi :
- De vider d'abord son fichier `usages.json` (`~/Library/Application Support/AI Usages Tracker/usages.json` ou équivalent — vérifie le path exact) pour repartir d'un état propre.
- Que sa fenêtre 5h reviendra à l'état "token_expired" au prochain poll après installation, donc il devra **relancer `claude` dans son terminal une fois** pour rafraîchir le bearer. Si après ça le fetch passe à 200 et qu'il voit ses % réels, le fix est validé.

Tu veux que je lance la voie 1 (script de création d'issue via la skill `assistant-propose`) ou tu préfères piloter à la main ?

# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : `puis ok pour voie 1`

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   User initially reported that multiple users see `???` in the app when using Claude Desktop without the Claude Code CLI, hypothesizing a token retrieval issue. After investigation and implementation of a comprehensive fix, the user asked how to share the build with Robin (one affected user). I provided two options: CI workflow via GitHub (recommended) or local build. The user confirmed they want to proceed with option 1 (CI workflow) and has asked me to commit the current changes.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - OAuth token lifecycle and expiry semantics (access token vs refresh token)
   - Stale-token failure mode: 401 → 429 cascade when expired bearer repeatedly hammers an endpoint
   - Local token expiry pre-check using `expiresAt` (Unix millisecond epoch) from macOS Keychain JSON
   - Sticky auth-failure flag to prevent 429 from masking the underlying auth diagnostic
   - Metric preservation across auth failures: token validity is independent of data validity
   - Swift Testing `.serialized` suite ordering and shared static state (`MockURLProtocol.handler`)
   - Gatekeeper bypass for ad-hoc-signed macOS apps (right-click → Open)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Connectors/ClaudeCredentialLocator.swift`:
     - Added `expiresAt` field parsing from keychain JSON
     - Added `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired(serviceName:expiredAt:)` case
     - Added static `parseExpiresAt(_:)` method accepting Double/Int/String (millisecond epoch)
     - Added 60-second expiry skew margin to avoid token-boundary races
     - Added injectable `clock` parameter for testing
   
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Connectors/ClaudeCodeConnector.swift`:
     - Added `lastAuthFailureSeen: Bool` private actor var to track sticky auth failure
     - Modified `fetchUsages()` to catch `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` and return error entry with preserved metrics
     - Modified HTTP 401 handling to return `token_expired` (not generic `http_401`), preserve metrics, mark `isActive: true`, arm the sticky flag
     - Modified HTTP 429 handling: if `lastAuthFailureSeen` is true, re-attribute to `token_expired` instead of `http_429`
     - Modified HTTP 200 success path to clear `lastAuthFailureSeen` flag
   
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/ClaudeCredentialLocatorTests.swift` (new file):
     - 8 tests covering token future/past/skew, missing/malformed `expiresAt`, keychain errors
   
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/ClaudeCodeConnectorTests.swift`:
     - Modified "HTTP 401" test to verify metrics are preserved, plus added cascading 429 test
     - Added "locator tokenExpired" test
     - Added 5 guard tests (prefixed `guard:`) testing per-field metric preservation across 401 and 429 cascades
     - Tests use future-dated payload (`2099-12-31`) to guard against accidental `resetAt`-based wipes
   
   - `docs/vendors/claude.md`:
     - Bumped "Last verified" to 2026-05-19
     - Documented `expiresAt` field format (Unix milliseconds)
     - Added section "Local expiry pre-check" explaining the `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` mechanism
     - Added new section "Stale-token failure mode" describing the 401→429 cascade, the sticky flag, and metrics preservation rationale
     - Updated Error catalog table with per-status outcome details
     - Updated Change log with detailed entry for 2026-05-19

4. Errors and fixes:
   - Initial fix overshoot: I first wiped `lastKnownMetrics` on 401 and `tokenExpired`. User correctly pointed out this deleted still-valid data (windows not yet expired). Fix: removed the wipe, preserved metrics, and added sticky flag to control 429 re-attribution instead.
   - Test suite parallelism issue: New guard tests in a sibling suite were failing and polluting the main suite via shared `MockURLProtocol.handler` state. Fix: moved all guard tests into the same `.serialized` suite, prefixed them `guard:`, and added explanatory comment about why they must stay in the same suite.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Diagnosed root cause: Robin logged in once (April 27), token expired within hours (May 19 = 22 days later), poll continued → Anthropic switched from 401 to 429 chronic. UI preserved metrics but showed `???` because `resetAt` was in past.
   - Designed three-part defensive fix: (a) local pre-check to avoid the HTTP call entirely, (b) preserve metrics so UI can degrade them per-row via `isUnknown` logic, (c) sticky flag to keep auth diagnostic across 429 cascade.
   - Resolved test isolation issue by recognizing Swift Testing's `.serialized` scoping and consolidating tests into one suite.

6. All user messages:
   - "j'ai plusieurs utilisateurs qui me remontent que lorsqu'ils n'utilisent pas claude code CLI mais uniquement claude desktop, leur fenêtre de temps ne démarre jamais..." [Multiple users report time-window never starts when using Claude Desktop only, not the CLI]
   - "Je pose l'hypothèse que c'est parce que je n'arrive pas à récupérer leur token d'authentification" [I hypothesize it's because I can't retrieve their auth token]
   - "Provided usages.json with lastError.type: http_429 and resetAt: 2026-04-27" [shows Robin's stale 429 state]
   - "l'app affiche des ???: /path/to/image.png" [The app displays ??? with screenshot showing degraded UI]
   - "Continue from where you left off" [Request to proceed]
   - "On est d'accords qu'avec un token expired, on ne supprime pas des données d'une time-window qui n'est pas elle-même expired?" [Clarification: with expired token, we don't delete non-expired window data?]
   - "tu pourrais rajouter des tests en garde fou pour éviter que des fenêtres non expirées soient supprimées par inadvertance" [Add guard tests to prevent accidental window deletion on token expiry]
   - "comment puis-je faire pour partager le build à robin?" [How can I share the build with Robin?]
   - Command: `commit` with intent `puis ok pour voie 1` [Commit the changes; proceed with CI workflow approach]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Create a `type:vendor-evolution` issue for this fix (Claude vendor stale-token handling)
   - Open a PR linked to that issue with `Closes #<n>` in description
   - Let the `assistant-build.yml` workflow produce and publish the DMG to the linked issue
   - Share the DMG download link with Robin
   - Robin to clear `usages.json`, re-run `claude` CLI to refresh token, test the app
   - Collect sign-off via `assistant-tester-followup` skill

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the `commit` request, the user asked how to share the build with Robin. I provided two approaches: (1) CI workflow via GitHub (recommended, produces a DMG published to an issue comment), or (2) local build via `./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` zipped and sent. The user chose option 1 ("voie 1") and now wants me to commit the code changes so they can proceed with creating the issue and PR.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Commit all staged changes with a clear message following the project's conventions. The commit should explain the three-part fix: local token expiry pre-check in the locator, explicit `token_expired` mapping in the connector for 401 and locator errors, sticky auth-failure flag to preserve the diagnostic across 429 cascades, and preserved metrics to let the UI degrade them per-row. After commit, the user can create the GitHub issue/PR to trigger the CI workflow and produce the DMG for Robin.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/fcamblor/.claude/projects/-Users-fcamblor-conductor-workspaces-mac-ai-trackers-damascus/ecf1199f-eae7-41ed-9976-ab0be6461952.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

---
paths: **/*.md
---

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-19 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-19_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":1715000000000}}`.
   `expiresAt` is a Unix millisecond timestamp (Node.js `Date.now()`
   convention — Claude Code CLI is a Node/Bun program).

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`. The locator also never performs the OAuth refresh
flow — if the access token has expired, the only way to get a new one
is for the user to invoke the `claude` CLI, which uses the stored
refresh token. **An access token whose owner stops using the CLI dies
within hours and stays dead until the CLI is invoked again** — see the
"Stale-token failure mode" section below.

### Local expiry pre-check

Before sending each request, the locator parses `expiresAt` from the
keychain JSON and short-circuits with `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` if
the token is past its expiry (with a 60-second skew margin to avoid
racing the boundary). A missing or unparseable `expiresAt` skips the
local check and falls through to the HTTP layer — a real 401 then
surfaces as `token_expired` (see Error catalog).

This pre-check exists specifically to break the chronic 401→429 loop
described in the Stale-token failure mode below.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Stale-token failure mode

_verified: 2026-05-19_

A user who logs into the `claude` CLI once (typically to enable this
app) and then stops using the CLI exhibits a distinctive failure mode:

1. The keychain `accessToken` expires within hours (no refresh because
   the CLI is never invoked).
2. Subsequent calls to `/api/oauth/usage` return HTTP 401.
3. If the app keeps polling on the expired token (every 15s), Anthropic
   eventually switches from 401 to **HTTP 429** (rate_limit_error). The
   429 persists indefinitely as long as the polling continues — it is
   not a transient throttle, it is a downstream effect of the 401.
4. Without a sticky auth-failure flag, each 429 overwrites the
   connector's `lastError` to `http_429` and the actionable diagnostic
   ("token expired, re-run the CLI") is lost behind a misleading
   "rate limited" surface.

The fix has three pieces:

- **Locator-side pre-check.** Parsing `expiresAt` and short-circuiting
  with `tokenExpired` skips the HTTP call entirely when we already know
  the bearer is dead, which prevents the request that would have
  produced the 429 cascade in the first place.
- **Preserve `lastKnownMetrics` on `token_expired`.** Token expiry
  invalidates our ability to *refresh* the numbers, not the numbers
  themselves. A 5h session at 42% is still 42% — the user actually
  consumed that. Values whose window has not yet rolled stay accurate
  until their `resetAt`; once `resetAt` is in the past, the UI's
  `isUnknown` logic naturally degrades the row to `???` without any
  data wipe on our side.
- **Sticky auth-failure flag.** Once a 401 (or local `tokenExpired`)
  has fired, subsequent 429s are re-attributed to `token_expired` so
  the auth signal is not overwritten by the cascade. The flag clears
  on any HTTP 200.

The fix only addresses the diagnostic, not the root cause: the user
must still re-invoke the `claude` CLI for the OAuth refresh to happen.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-19_

| Source | Outcome | Connector response |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` (pre-check) | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag; skip HTTP entirely |
| Locator | other `ClaudeAuthError` (keychain missing / denied / parse) | surface `token_error` |
| HTTP   | 200 | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`, clear sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 401 | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 429 (auth-failure flag armed) | re-attribute to `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | 429 (no recent auth failure) | surface `http_429`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | other | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if a 200 response cannot be decoded as JSON
or contains no known window block.

`lastKnownMetrics` is intentionally **preserved** across auth failures:
an expired bearer means we can no longer refresh the data, but the
values we previously read are still semantically valid until their
window's `resetAt` naturally lapses. Wiping them would destroy
information that is still correct; instead the UI's `isUnknown` logic
collapses each metric to `???` on its own schedule (per-row), while
the `token_expired` error type drives any global "re-auth required"
banner.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.
- 2026-05-19 — documented `expiresAt` keychain field (Unix ms epoch),
  added local expiry pre-check in the locator and explicit
  `token_expired` mapping for both the pre-check and HTTP 401.
  `lastKnownMetrics` is preserved across auth failures (data validity
  is independent of token validity — the UI's `isUnknown` logic
  handles per-row staleness on `resetAt` lapse). A sticky
  auth-failure flag re-attributes subsequent 429s to `token_expired`
  until a successful 200 disarms it, preventing the chronic 401→429
  cascade from masking the auth diagnostic. Documented the
  Stale-token failure mode that motivated the change.

skills.name: commit
skills.content:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : `puis ok pour voie 1`

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

The user is asking me to commit the changes. Looking at the git status provided in the system message:

```

 [31mM[m docs/vendors/claude.md
[31m??[m AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/ClaudeCredentialLocatorTests.swift
```

So there are modified files and one new untracked file. The user's intent is "puis ok pour voie 1" (then ok for approach 1), which confirms they want to proceed with committing for the CI workflow approach.

The commit skill has already been invoked earlier in the session. The instructions say:
- Don't re-execute skills or perform their one-time setup actions again
- Continue to apply ongoing behavioral guidelines from these skills where still relevant

However, this is a fresh request from the user to commit. They're now explicitly asking to commit ("ok pour voie 1" = commit and proceed). I should follow the commit skill guidelines:

1. Analyze repo state
2. Detect conventions
3. Detect fixup (unlikely here)
4. Write message explaining WHY
5. Stage and commit
6. Verify

Looking at the recent commits from git status:
```
[33m2edda4f[m feat(codex-status): filter incidents by subscribed Codex components (#42)
[33mb9ef463[m ci: add Swift build + test workflow on macos-15 (#40)
[33m46c3c02[m fix(menubar): stop status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU (#39)


[33m447aad8[m fix(menubar): use single source of truth for dot color when percent unknown (#38)
[33mff3bc98[m fix(ci): pass --repo to gh release list in cleanup-on-close
```

This is Conventional Commits in English. The pattern is `type(scope): message`.

The changes implement token expiry pre-checking in the locator, token expired error mapping in the connector while preserving metrics, a sticky auth-failure flag to prevent 401→429 cascades, and test guards for metrics preservation. This is a fix for the stale-token failure mode, so the scope would be "claude-connector" or "auth".

The issue: when a user logs in once and stops using the CLI, the token expires within hours but polling continues, causing Anthropic to return 429 responses instead of 401s. This leaves Robin seeing `???` in the UI. The solution adds a local token expiry pre-check using the keychain JSON field (Unix milliseconds), preserves lastKnownMetrics across auth failures, and uses a sticky flag to maintain auth diagnostics through the cascade.

The commit includes test coverage for expiry boundaries, skew margins, malformed expiresAt fields, and keychain errors, plus updates to metric preservation across 401/429 scenarios. Documentation gets updated with the full semantics and failure mode explanation.

Current git state shows modified files across the connector, locator, tests, and docs—all part of the same feature and should be committed together. Need to check git status and stage everything before proceeding with the issue/PR workflow.

content.content:
# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-19 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-19_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":1715000000000}}`.
   `expiresAt` is a Unix millisecond timestamp (Node.js `Date.now()`
   convention — Claude Code CLI is a Node/Bun program).

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`. The locator also never performs the OAuth refresh
flow — if the access token has expired, the only way to get a new one
is for the user to invoke the `claude` CLI, which uses the stored
refresh token. **An access token whose owner stops using the CLI dies
within hours and stays dead until the CLI is invoked again** — see the
"Stale-token failure mode" section below.

### Local expiry pre-check

Before sending each request, the locator parses `expiresAt` from the
keychain JSON and short-circuits with `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` if
the token is past its expiry (with a 60-second skew margin to avoid
racing the boundary). A missing or unparseable `expiresAt` skips the
local check and falls through to the HTTP layer — a real 401 then
surfaces as `token_expired` (see Error catalog).

This pre-check exists specifically to break the chronic 401→429 loop
described in the Stale-token failure mode below.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Stale-token failure mode

_verified: 2026-05-19_

A user who logs into the `claude` CLI once (typically to enable this
app) and then stops using the CLI exhibits a distinctive failure mode:

1. The keychain `accessToken` expires within hours (no refresh because
   the CLI is never invoked).
2. Subsequent calls to `/api/oauth/usage` return HTTP 401.
3. If the app keeps polling on the expired token (every 15s), Anthropic
   eventually switches from 401 to **HTTP 429** (rate_limit_error). The
   429 persists indefinitely as long as the polling continues — it is
   not a transient throttle, it is a downstream effect of the 401.
4. Without a sticky auth-failure flag, each 429 overwrites the
   connector's `lastError` to `http_429` and the actionable diagnostic
   ("token expired, re-run the CLI") is lost behind a misleading
   "rate limited" surface.

The fix has three pieces:

- **Locator-side pre-check.** Parsing `expiresAt` and short-circuiting
  with `tokenExpired` skips the HTTP call entirely when we already know
  the bearer is dead, which prevents the request that would have
  produced the 429 cascade in the first place.
- **Preserve `lastKnownMetrics` on `token_expired`.** Token expiry
  invalidates our ability to *refresh* the numbers, not the numbers
  themselves. A 5h session at 42% is still 42% — the user actually
  consumed that. Values whose window has not yet rolled stay accurate
  until their `resetAt`; once `resetAt` is in the past, the UI's
  `isUnknown` logic naturally degrades the row to `???` without any
  data wipe on our side.
- **Sticky auth-failure flag.** Once a 401 (or local `tokenExpired`)
  has fired, subsequent 429s are re-attributed to `token_expired` so
  the auth signal is not overwritten by the cascade. The flag clears
  on any HTTP 200.

The fix only addresses the diagnostic, not the root cause: the user
must still re-invoke the `claude` CLI for the OAuth refresh to happen.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-19_

| Source | Outcome | Connector response |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` (pre-check) | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag; skip HTTP entirely |
| Locator | other `ClaudeAuthError` (keychain missing / denied / parse) | surface `token_error` |
| HTTP   | 200 | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`, clear sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 401 | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 429 (auth-failure flag armed) | re-attribute to `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | 429 (no recent auth failure) | surface `http_429`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | other | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if a 200 response cannot be decoded as JSON
or contains no known window block.

`lastKnownMetrics` is intentionally **preserved** across auth failures:
an expired bearer means we can no longer refresh the data, but the
values we previously read are still semantically valid until their
window's `resetAt` naturally lapses. Wiping them would destroy
information that is still correct; instead the UI's `isUnknown` logic
collapses each metric to `???` on its own schedule (per-row), while
the `token_expired` error type drives any global "re-auth required"
banner.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.
- 2026-05-19 — documented `expiresAt` keychain field (Unix ms epoch),
  added local expiry pre-check in the locator and explicit
  `token_expired` mapping for both the pre-check and HTTP 401.
  `lastKnownMetrics` is preserved across auth failures (data validity
  is independent of token validity — the UI's `isUnknown` logic
  handles per-row staleness on `resetAt` lapse). A sticky
  auth-failure flag re-attributes subsequent 429s to `token_expired`
  until a successful 200 disarms it, preventing the chronic 401→429
  cascade from masking the auth diagnostic. Documented the
  Stale-token failure mode that motivated the change.

surfaces as `token_expired` (see Error catalog).

This pre-check exists specifically to break the chronic 401→429 loop
described in the Stale-token failure mode below.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Stale-token failure mode

_verified: 2026-05-19_

A user who logs into the `claude` CLI once (typically to enable this
app) and then stops using the CLI exhibits a distinctive failure mode:

1. The keychain `accessToken` expires within hours (no refresh because
   the CLI is never invoked).
2. Subsequent calls to `/api/oauth/usage` return HTTP 401.
3. If the app keeps polling on the expired token (every 15s), Anthropic
   eventually switches from 401 to **HTTP 429** (rate_limit_error). The
   429 persists indefinitely as long as the polling continues — it is
   not a transient throttle, it is a downstream effect of the 401.
4. Without a sticky auth-failure flag, each 429 overwrites the
   connector's `lastError` to `http_429` and the actionable diagnostic
   ("token expired, re-run the CLI") is lost behind a misleading
   "rate limited" surface.

The fix has three pieces:

- **Locator-side pre-check.** Parsing `expiresAt` and short-circuiting
  with `tokenExpired` skips the HTTP call entirely when we already know
  the bearer is dead, which prevents the request that would have
  produced the 429 cascade in the first place.
- **Preserve `lastKnownMetrics` on `token_expired`.** Token expiry
  invalidates our ability to *refresh* the numbers, not the numbers
  themselves. A 5h session at 42% is still 42% — the user actually
  consumed that. Values whose window has not yet rolled stay accurate
  until their `resetAt`; once `resetAt` is in the past, the UI's
  `isUnknown` logic naturally degrades the row to `???` without any
  data wipe on our side.
- **Sticky auth-failure flag.** Once a 401 (or local `tokenExpired`)
  has fired, subsequent 429s are re-attributed to `token_expired` so
  the auth signal is not overwritten by the cascade. The flag clears
  on any HTTP 200.

The fix only addresses the diagnostic, not the root cause: the user
must still re-invoke the `claude` CLI for the OAuth refresh to happen.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,

evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-19_

| Source | Outcome | Connector response |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` (pre-check) | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag; skip HTTP entirely |
| Locator | other `ClaudeAuthError` (keychain missing / denied / parse) | surface `token_error` |
| HTTP   | 200 | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`, clear sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 401 | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 429 (auth-failure flag armed) | re-attribute to `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | 429 (no recent auth failure) | surface `http_429`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | other | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if a 200 response cannot be decoded as JSON
or contains no known window block.

`lastKnownMetrics` is intentionally **preserved** across auth failures:
an expired bearer means we can no longer refresh the data, but the
values we previously read are still semantically valid until their
window's `resetAt` naturally lapses. Wiping them would destroy
information that is still correct; instead the UI's `isUnknown` logic
collapses each metric to `???` on its own schedule (per-row), while
the `token_expired` error type drives any global "re-auth required"
banner.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.
- 2026-05-19 — documented `expiresAt` keychain field (Unix ms epoch),
  added local expiry pre-check in the locator and explicit
  `token_expired` mapping for both the pre-check and HTTP 401.
  `lastKnownMetrics` is preserved across auth failures (data validity
  is independent of token validity — the UI's `isUnknown` logic
  handles per-row staleness on `resetAt` lapse). A sticky
  auth-failure flag re-attributes subsequent 429s to `token_expired`
  until a successful 200 disarms it, preventing the chronic 401→429
  cascade from masking the auth diagnostic. Documented the
  Stale-token failure mode that motivated the change.

in once and later stops using the CLI (token expires within hours, polling
continues, Anthropic switches to 429).

  epoch). Short-circuits with tokenExpired, skipping the HTTP request that
  would trigger the cascade.

  not on token expiry. Metrics stay correct until their window naturally
  expires.

  of http_429, keeping the auth diagnostic actionable and preventing the
  cascade from masking the underlying issue.

60-second skew margin, missing/malformed expiresAt, and keychain errors.

preservation across 401 and 429 cascades.
